sentence: Subjective:
HPI: Ashley, a 14-year-old female, presents for evaluation of abnormal renal ultrasound with an atrophic right kidney.

She was previously healthy but recently experienced severe abdominal pain and was diagnosed with atrophic right kidney and cysts on her kidney and ovaries, along with swollen lymph nodes and infection.
She has a history of recurrent infections, starting at 4 years old, with symptoms of fever and vomiting, but no underlying cause was identified.
Her father had a kidney transplant due to obstructive neuropathy and died at 47 due to complications with dialysis.
Her aunt has end-stage renal disease, chronic kidney disease, stage V, diabetes, and hypertension.
Past medical history: Ashley has a history of recurrent infections starting at 4 years old, with symptoms of fever and vomiting, but no underlying cause was identified.
She has been treated for a stomach virus with fluids and antibiotics.
Her father had a kidney transplant due to obstructive neuropathy and died at 47 due to complications with dialysis.
Her aunt has end-stage renal disease, chronic kidney disease, stage V, diabetes, and hypertension.
Review of systems: Ashley complains of abdominal pain, particularly on the right side, and burning sensation when urinating, especially after consuming certain drinks like soda.
Current medications: Ashley takes 40 mg of Pepcid daily for a stomach condition and 1 tablet of Claritin daily for allergies.
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: Ashley appears well-appearing, alert, and oriented, with no acute distress.
Her physical exam is within normal limits.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: Ashley has an atrophic right kidney and a history of recurrent infections, which may have contributed to her current kidney condition.
Problem list: Atrophic right kidney; Recurrent infections; Potential for kidney disease; Hypertension; Proteinuria; Family history of kidney disease.
Plan:

I recommended that Ashley have an annual ultrasound to monitor the size of her kidneys and their overall function, complete a urinalysis to ensure protein and blood are not present in her urine, and have a blood pressure check.
I advised her to avoid nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory medicines like ibuprofen and use Tylenol instead.
I also recommended that she stay hydrated by drinking at least 2 liters of water per day, maintain a healthy diet, and continue her active lifestyle to help prevent kidney damage.
Follow-up:
Ashley can schedule her next appointment with her primary care physician or an adult nephrologist closer to her home, as an alternative to traveling to Atlanta.
